objects.claim: Troglitazone, as a PPARγ agonist, suppresses LPS-induced microglial production of pro-inflammatory molecules and prevents LPS-induced dopaminergic neuronal death in vitro.

pathway_edge.target_node: Reduced microglial pro-inflammatory output → reduced LPS-induced dopaminergic neuronal death

study_context.species: in vitro (cell culture, implied rodent microglia/neurons)

source.quoted_span: synthetic agonists of PPAR-γ, including troglitazone, ciglitazone and pioglitazone, can also suppress the excessive production of pro-inflammatory molecules and prevent LPS-induced neuronal death (Kim et al., 2002; Hunter et al., 2007).

limitations: Troglitazone cited as one of three co-tested compounds; troglitazone-specific effect sizes not disaggregated from pioglitazone/ciglitazone in the excerpt
Underlying primary study (Kim et al. 2002) not directly retrieved; attribution mediated via review article
Troglitazone withdrawn from clinical use (hepatotoxicity, 2000) — translational relevance for human PD is limited

objects.claim: PPARγ agonism (TZD class) reduces neuroinflammation in a mitochondrial complex-IV dopaminergic neuron knockout PD model (Cox10/DAT-cre mouse) and improves motor phenotype, but without restoring mitochondrial function or dopaminergic neuron number, indicating a purely anti-inflammatory rather than mitochondria-rescuing mechanism.

pathway_edge.target_node: Motor phenotype improvement WITHOUT dopaminergic neuron rescue or mitochondrial restoration

study_context.dose: chronic pioglitazone (dose not specified in excerpt)

source.quoted_span: Although neither mitochondrial function nor the number of dopaminergic neurons was improved, neuroinflammation in the midbrain and the striatum was decreased.

limitations: Troglitazone not directly tested; class extrapolation from pioglitazone
Genetic complex-IV knockout model is a specific mitochondrial PD model; does not represent idiopathic PD or toxin-driven pathology
Lack of dopaminergic neuron rescue with PPARγ agonism questions disease-modification vs. symptomatic benefit

objects.claim: The PPARγ agonism responsible for troglitazone's putative neuroprotection in the MPTP mouse model may be pharmacologically confounded by MAO-B inhibition (demonstrated for pioglitazone), which blocks MPTP→MPP⁺ bioactivation independently of anti-inflammatory effects.

chemical_identity.name: Troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent thiazolidinedione (class; confound demonstrated for pioglitazone)

pathway_edge.target_node: Blocked MPTP→MPP⁺ conversion → apparent neuroprotection (model artifact)

source.quoted_span: The neuroprotection observed with pioglitazone pretreatment in the MPTP mouse model was due to the blockade of the conversion of MPTP to its active toxic metabolite MPP+, via inhibition of MAO-B.

limitations: This confound is demonstrated for pioglitazone; troglitazone's MAO-B inhibitory potency has not been characterised in the retrieved evidence
MAO-B confound applies only to MPTP-based models, not 6-OHDA, rotenone, or human disease
Anti-inflammatory PPARγ effects may still contribute to neuroprotection in non-MPTP models

objects.claim: Troglitazone was clinically withdrawn due to hepatotoxicity (liver failure) in 2000, which constitutes a critical counterevidence limiting its therapeutic development for any indication including PD, and means that any PD-relevant signal demonstrated in preclinical models cannot currently be translated to human therapy.

pathway_edge.target_node: No viable human PD therapeutic development pathway

source.quoted_span: synthetic agonists of PPAR-γ, including troglitazone, ciglitazone and pioglitazone, can also suppress the excessive production of pro-inflammatory molecules and prevent LPS-induced neuronal death (Kim et al., 2002; Hunter et al., 2007).

limitations: The cited review source names troglitazone in a neuroprotection context without discussing withdrawal; the hepatotoxicity fact is established pharmacological/regulatory knowledge (FDA withdrawal March 2000) but no dedicated primary source with verbatim text was retrieved for this specific claim
Hepatotoxicity mechanism (mitochondrial uncoupling, reactive quinone metabolite formation) is biochemically distinct from PPARγ neuroprotective mechanism — both can co-exist in the same compound
Structural analogues without the hepatotoxic chromane moiety could potentially preserve PPARγ agonism without liver liability

objects.claim: Troglitazone, acting via PPARγ, inhibits synthesis of pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, NO, and COX-2 in the CNS, thereby attenuating neurotoxicity relevant to PD neuroinflammation.

pathway_edge.target_node: Suppression of TNF-α / NO / COX-2 → attenuation of neurotoxicity

study_context.species: in vitro (rodent-derived CNS cells implied by cited literature)

source.quoted_span: Activation of PPARs inhibits the synthesis of pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, NO, cyclooxygenase-2 and related chemokines, to attenuate neurotoxicity (Bernardo et al., 2000; Kim et al., 2002).

limitations: Mechanistic statement covers the PPARγ agonist class broadly, not troglitazone exclusively
Troglitazone-specific data for CNS TNF-α/NO/COX-2 suppression not directly provided in the retrieved excerpt
The Frontiers article (2016) is a review; the original mechanistic data originate in Kim et al. 2002 (not independently retrieved here)

objects.claim: Activation of PPARγ by TZD agonists (including troglitazone-class molecules) increases PGC-1α expression, restores mitochondrial number and ultrastructure, and prevents MPP⁺/MPTP-induced dopaminergic neuronal damage in PD models.

chemical_identity.name: Troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent thiazolidinedione (structurally and mechanistically analogous to 2,4-TZD and pioglitazone tested in cited study)

pathway_edge.target_node: Mitochondrial biogenesis/fusion rescue → dopaminergic neuron survival (MPTP/MPP+ model)

study_context.tissue: substantia nigra / striatum (in vivo); dopaminergic cell line (in vitro)
study_context.dose: oral pioglitazone / 2,4-TZD (dose not specified in excerpt)

source.quoted_span: The results from this study confirm that pioglitazone/2,4-thiazolidinedione is able to activate PGC-1α and prevent damage of dopaminergic neurons and restore mitochondria ultrastructure through the regulation of mitochondria function.

limitations: Primary study tested pioglitazone and 2,4-TZD, not troglitazone itself; troglitazone shares the TZD scaffold and PPARγ mechanism but has additional metabolic/hepatotoxic liabilities
Mitochondrial rescue quantified by ultrastructural and PGC-1α endpoints — functional neuronal rescue confirmed behaviourally but troglitazone-specific EC50 not established
MPTP/MPP+ model does not fully recapitulate sporadic PD pathology (e.g., no α-synuclein pathology)

objects.claim: PPARγ agonism (TZD class) inhibits the apoptotic cascade in MPP⁺-treated dopaminergic neurons by restoring Bcl-2 and ERK expression and reducing Bax, implicating troglitazone-class compounds in anti-apoptotic neuroprotection in PD.

pathway_edge.target_node: Reduced apoptosis in MPP⁺-treated dopaminergic neurons

study_context.species: mouse (in vivo MPTP) / cell line (in vitro MPP⁺)

source.quoted_span: We show that protein levels of Bcl-2 and ERK were reduced in the MPP+-treated group compared with the control group. This effect was observed to be reversed upon treatment with 2,4-thiazolidinedione, as Bcl-2 and ERK expression levels were increased. We also observed that levels of the apoptotic protein Bax showed opposite changes compared to Bcl-2 and ERK levels.

limitations: Direct data for troglitazone not provided; extrapolated from 2,4-TZD scaffold-level class evidence
In vitro MPP⁺ model: cell line context, not primary neurons
Bcl-2/Bax ratio as endpoint does not distinguish neuroprotection from cytostasis

objects.claim: PPARγ agonism in the MPTP mouse model protects TH-positive substantia nigra neurons from death via NF-κB/IκBα/iNOS pathway suppression — a mechanistic pathway shared by troglitazone-class TZDs.

chemical_identity.name: Troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent thiazolidinedione (class; direct data from pioglitazone)

pathway_edge.source_node: PPARγ agonism → IκBα induction / NF-κB & iNOS blockade
pathway_edge.target_node: TH-positive SNpc neuron survival (MPTP model)

source.quoted_span: Inflammation has been implicated in the pathogenesis of Parkinson's disease (PD). In the chronic 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) model of PD, inducible NO synthase (iNOS) derived nitric oxide (NO) is an important mediator of dopaminergic cell death.

limitations: Direct data on troglitazone's NF-κB/iNOS effects in MPTP not retrieved; pathway established by pioglitazone in this PMID
iNOS-derived NO as mediator is established in MPTP model but its relative weight in human sporadic PD remains uncertain
Protective effect of pioglitazone on striatal dopamine was only partial in this study

objects.claim: PPARγ is expressed in both the striatum and substantia nigra in vivo, validating it as an anatomically appropriate target for troglitazone-class agonism in the brain regions most affected in PD; oral TZD agonism at this receptor protected TH-positive SNpc neurons in the MPTP model.

chemical_identity.name: Troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent thiazolidinedione (class; receptor-level data from pioglitazone/MPTP study)

pathway_edge.target_node: TH-positive neuron survival under MPTP neurotoxic challenge

source.quoted_span: Oral administration of 20 mg/(kg day) of pioglitazone protected tyrosine hydroxylase (TH)-positive substantia nigra neurons from death induced by 5 x 30 mg/kg MPTP. However, the decrease of dopamine in the striatum was only partially prevented.

limitations: Troglitazone not directly tested; PPARγ receptor expression confirmed for pioglitazone studies
Partial dopamine protection in striatum suggests incomplete neuroprotection at the terminal level
MPTP model may not capture the full mechanistic spectrum of human idiopathic PD

objects.claim: In MPTP-treated rhesus monkeys, oral PPARγ agonism (pioglitazone 5 mg/kg) preserved nigrostriatal TH-positive and VMAT-2-positive neurons and reduced CD68-positive neuroinflammatory cells, providing primate-level mechanistic grounding for the TZD class that includes troglitazone.

chemical_identity.name: Troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent thiazolidinedione (class; NHP data from pioglitazone)

pathway_edge.target_node: Preservation of TH/VMAT-2 nigrostriatal neurons (MPTP NHP model)

study_context.dose: pioglitazone 2.5 or 5 mg/kg/day oral × 3 months; MPTP 3 mg intracarotid

source.quoted_span: Behavioral recovery was associated with preservation of nigrostriatal dopaminergic markers, observed as higher tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) putaminal optical density (P = 0.011), higher stereological cell counts of TH-ir (P = 0.02) and vesicular monoamine transporter-2 (VMAT-2)-ir nigral neurons (P = 0.006).

limitations: Troglitazone not used; class extrapolation from pioglitazone NHP data
Intracarotid MPTP administration is not a standard route (acute rather than chronic dopaminergic lesion)
Troglitazone was withdrawn from the market in 2000; clinical translation is not feasible without reformulation

objects.claim: A meta-analysis of real-world evidence (PubMed/Embase/Cochrane through September 2019) found that TZD use in type 2 diabetes patients is associated with reduced PD risk, with the primary outcome being the pooled hazard ratio of PD among TZD users versus non-users.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class-level meta-analysis; troglitazone is a founding TZD but was not the dominant agent in any contributing post-2000 cohort

pathway_edge.target_node: incident PD risk (pooled HR, reduced)

study_context.tissue: population (multiple observational cohorts, type 2 DM patients)

source.quoted_span: The primary outcome was the pooled hazard ratio (HR) of PD among type 2 DM TZD users as compared with TZD non-users in people with diabetes.

limitations: Meta-analysis aggregates pioglitazone and rosiglitazone predominantly; troglitazone-specific data not isolated
Troglitazone withdrawn in 2000 and is virtually absent from all post-2000 cohorts
Residual confounding by indication in all contributing observational studies
Heterogeneity across contributing studies not fully resolved
Cannot attribute the pooled protective signal specifically to troglitazone

objects.claim: In a Taiwanese retrospective cohort of 38,521 newly-diagnosed DM patients (2001–2013), TZD use was associated with significantly lower PD risk (HR = 0.399 vs. non-TZD group), with a dose-dependent inverse relationship (HRs ranging from 0.613 to 0.081 for cumulative defined daily doses from 0–90 to >720).

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class (predominantly pioglitazone/rosiglitazone post-2001 in Taiwan; troglitazone not available during cohort period 2001–2013)

pathway_edge.target_node: incident PD diagnosis (inverse dose–response)

study_context.tissue: population (National Health Insurance Research Dataset, Taiwan)

source.quoted_span: In the study cohort, the risk of PD was significantly lower in the thiazolidinedione-treated group (HR = 0.399) compared to the non-thiazolidinedione-treated group. Thiazolidinedione reduced the risk of PD in a dose-dependent manner, with HRs ranging from 0.613 to 0.081 with defined daily doses of 0-90 to >720, respectively.

limitations: Cohort enrolled 2001–2013; troglitazone withdrawn globally by 2000 and unavailable in Taiwan during study period
Protective dose–response signal attributable exclusively to pioglitazone/rosiglitazone, not troglitazone
Possible surveillance bias and reverse causality (prodromal PD patients may avoid initiating new medications)
Active comparator not used; non-TZD diabetic group as reference introduces potential confounding

objects.claim: A mainland Chinese retrospective cohort (Yinzhou Regional Health Care Database) comprising 12,704 TZD users and 49,696 alpha-glucosidase inhibitor (AGI) users found PD incidence of 135 per 100,000 person-years in TZD users versus 203 per 100,000 person-years in the AGI group, with IPTW-adjusted Cox modeling suggesting a protective association.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class (pioglitazone dominant in China cohort; troglitazone was never approved in China)

pathway_edge.target_node: incident PD (reduced incidence vs. AGI comparator)

source.quoted_span: The incidence of PD was 135 per 100,000 person-years in TZD users and 203 per 100,000 person-years in the AGI group.

limitations: Troglitazone was never approved in China; finding is entirely attributable to other TZDs (pioglitazone)
IPTW-based confounding control may be incomplete for unmeasured confounders
Human TZD–PD study results described as 'conflicting due to methodology heterogeneity, population difference, and various follow-up periods'
PD prodromal period (~5–10 years) may exceed cohort follow-up, causing underestimation or misclassification

objects.claim: Brauer et al. (2015, PLoS Medicine) conducted a UK-based retrospective cohort study examining glitazone treatment and incidence of Parkinson's disease among people with diabetes; this study is cited across multiple meta-analyses as finding glitazones associated with reduced PD risk, but enrolled post-2000 patients using pioglitazone/rosiglitazone exclusively (troglitazone had been withdrawn).

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class; Brauer 2015 enrolled UK diabetic patients exposed to glitazones post-2000, so pioglitazone/rosiglitazone only; troglitazone excluded by withdrawal timing

pathway_edge.target_node: incident PD (reduced incidence in diabetic cohort)

study_context.tissue: population (UK diabetic patients, retrospective cohort, PLoS Med 2015)

source.quoted_span: Brauer R, Bhaskaran K, Chaturvedi N, Dexter DT, Smeeth L, Douglas I (2015) Glitazone treatment and incidence of Parkinson's disease among people with diabetes: a retrospective cohort study. PLoS Med 12:e1001854

limitations: Citation identified via meta-analysis reference list; primary Brauer 2015 full text not directly accessed in this search
Glitazone exposure post-2000 structurally excludes troglitazone from this cohort
Specific HR and 95% CI for Brauer 2015 not extractable from the accessed excerpt
Confounding by indication inherent in all retrospective diabetic cohort designs

objects.claim: In a propensity-score-matched cohort of 29,397 Medicare patients, thiazolidinedione (TZD) users had a hazard ratio of 1.09 (95% CI: 0.71–1.66) for Parkinson disease compared with sulfonylurea users, indicating no statistically significant protective or harmful association.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class-level analysis; troglitazone included among TZD users (cohort enrolled 1997–2005, overlapping troglitazone's market period)

pathway_edge.target_node: incident Parkinson disease diagnosis

source.quoted_span: In the primary analysis, thiazolidinedione users had a hazard ratio for a diagnosis of Parkinson disease of 1.09 (95% confidence interval: 0.71, 1.66) when compared with sulfonylurea users.

limitations: Class-level analysis; troglitazone and other TZDs not disaggregated
Troglitazone withdrawn in 2000; cohort 1997–2005 includes only partial troglitazone-specific person-time before withdrawal
Active comparator (sulfonylurea) design may partially attenuate an absolute protective signal
Follow-up period short relative to the PD prodromal window (~5–10 years)
HR crossed null; no duration-response relationship detected in any sensitivity analysis

objects.claim: Only pioglitazone and rosiglitazone are currently in clinical use since troglitazone was withdrawn due to hepatotoxicity, rendering troglitazone absent from all post-2000 pharmacoepidemiological cohorts and FAERS-based pharmacovigilance studies assessing TZD–PD associations; no specific parkinsonism signal for troglitazone is detectable in those databases.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound; withdrawn from US market March 2000 due to idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity (clinical dose range: 200–600 mg/day oral)

pathway_edge.target_node: absence of troglitazone from post-2000 pharmacoepidemiological PD cohorts and FAERS parkinsonism signal detection

study_context.dose: clinical: 200–600 mg/day oral (approved range prior to withdrawal)

source.quoted_span: Only pioglitazone and rosiglitazone are currently in clinical use since troglitazone was withdrawn due to hepatotoxicity.

limitations: This is a contextual/regulatory observation embedded in a review, not a pharmacovigilance outcome study
Troglitazone's hepatotoxicity dominates its FAERS adverse-event profile, precluding sensitive detection of a low-frequency neurological signal
No FAERS-specific parkinsonism disproportionality analysis for troglitazone was identified in available sources
Short market duration (1997–2000 in US) limits statistical power for any rare-event signal

objects.claim: Preclinical studies indicate that TZDs have neuroprotective effects against PD through stimulating PPAR-γ, but debate remains in human studies due to conflicting results attributed to methodology heterogeneity, population differences, and various follow-up periods; this mechanistic hypothesis grounds the class-level epidemiological literature but cannot be attributed to troglitazone specifically in humans.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: TZD class (PPAR-γ agonist; troglitazone is a founding member of the pharmacological class)

pathway_edge.target_node: neuroprotection vs. PD (class-level mechanistic hypothesis)

source.quoted_span: Preclinical studies indicate that thiazolidinediones (TZDs) have neuroprotective effects against PD through stimulating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma. Nevertheless, debate remains in human studies.

limitations: Mechanistic grounding applies to the TZD class broadly; troglitazone-specific PPAR-γ neuroprotection not separately characterized in human clinical or epidemiological studies
One RCT (NET-PD FS-ZONE, cited in multiple reviews) found pioglitazone unlikely to slow PD progression in early, non-diabetic PD patients, weakening the translational link
Human epidemiological results for TZD class remain conflicting; causal inference is not established

objects.claim: Troglitazone was withdrawn from clinical use in 2000 due to idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity and was superseded by pioglitazone and rosiglitazone, which share the TZD-PPARγ pharmacophore but lack the chromane moiety — establishing the chromane arm as a structural differentiator for any troglitazone-specific toxicological or neuroprotective mechanism.

pathway_edge.target_node: Idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity / reactive quinone metabolite formation

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone was withdrawn in 2000 due to risk of hepatotoxicity. It was superseded by pioglitazone and rosiglitazone.

limitations: BioGRID chemical page is a database record, not a primary pharmacovigilance study
Withdrawal was for hepatotoxicity, not for CNS/PD-related effects; the counterevidence role here is that systemic toxicity limits therapeutic repurposing for PD
Mechanistic link between quinone metabolite and hepatotoxicity vs. neuroprotection requires primary mechanistic studies (not retrievable in this search)

objects.claim: Troglitazone (DTXSID8023719; CAS 97322-87-7; InChIKey GXPHKUHSUJUWKP-UHFFFAOYSA-N) is a thiazolidinedione PPARγ/PPARα agonist with molecular formula C24H27NO5S and MW 441.54, bearing a chromane (benzopyran) moiety structurally homologous to α-tocopherol that confers redox-active phenolic chemistry distinct from rosiglitazone and pioglitazone.

pathway_edge.target_node: PPARγ/PPARα activation AND redox-active phenolic antioxidant activity

source.quoted_span: SMILES:: CC1=C(C)C2=C(CCC(C)(COC3=CC=C(CC4SC(=O)NC4=O)C=C3)O2)C(C)=C1O

limitations: BioGRID chemical page is a database aggregation entry, not primary literature; identifiers cross-validated against Wikidata (DTXCID603719) and ZINC (DTXSID8023719)
Stereochemistry: C5 of TZD is a stereocenter; racemate used clinically but individual enantiomers may differ in PPARγ affinity
No PMID available for this structural grounding entry

objects.claim: Troglitazone contains an α-tocopherol (vitamin E-like) moiety — specifically the 6-hydroxy-2,5,7,8-tetramethyl chroman ring — in addition to the thiazolidinedione PPARγ-binding head group, potentially conferring vitamin E-like antioxidant activity relevant to dopaminergic neuroprotection in PD.

pathway_edge.target_node: Reactive oxygen species scavenging / lipid peroxidation inhibition

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone also contains an α-Tocopherol moiety, potentially giving it vitamin E-like activity in additi

limitations: Wikipedia is a secondary/tertiary source; the α-tocopherol structural homology is chemically unambiguous from SMILES but the functional claim (vitamin E-like antioxidant activity in vivo) requires primary literature corroboration
The chromanol can act as pro-oxidant (quinone methide formation) as well as antioxidant depending on CYP metabolic context
Truncated quote due to content extraction limit

objects.claim: Troglitazone is a ligand to both PPARα and — more strongly — PPARγ; PPARγ activation in microglia and astrocytes is a recognized neuroprotective mechanism with relevance to PD neuroinflammation, making PPARγ agonism the primary candidate pathway edge for any troglitazone-PD mechanistic investigation.

pathway_edge.target_node: Neuroinflammation suppression (NF-κB inhibition, microglial M2 polarization)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone, like the other thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone and rosiglitazone), works by activating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors (PPARs). Troglitazone is a ligand to both PPARα and – more strongly – PPARγ.

limitations: Wikipedia is a secondary source; PPAR pharmacology is well-established but primary receptor-binding Ki/EC50 data needed for quantitative edge strength

The claim that PPARγ agonism is neuroprotective in PD is an inference from the drug class (supported by pioglitazone PD literature); direct troglitazone-specific neurological primary studies were not retrieved in this search
Troglitazone's CNS penetration is not annotated here and limits translation of peripheral PPAR agonism to brain targets

objects.claim: Troglitazone protects neuronal cells against prion peptide PrP(106–126)-induced neurotoxicity via a PPARγ–autophagy pathway, reducing apoptosis and restoring autophagic flux in a neurodegeneration model.

pathway_edge.target_node: autophagy flux restoration / reduced apoptosis (KE→AO)

source.quoted_span: The present study examined the effect of troglitazone, a PPARγ agonist, on autophagy flux in a prion peptide (PrP)(106–126)-mediated neurodegeneration model.

limitations: Prion peptide model ≠ PD model; no dopaminergic specificity shown; in vitro only; SH-SY5Y cells are of adrenal origin

objects.claim: Troglitazone (10 µM) significantly reduces LPS-stimulated iNOS expression and nitric oxide production in BV2 microglial cells via PPARγ, positioning it as a suppressor of microglial activation—a key KE in the neuroinflammatory arm of PD pathogenesis.

pathway_edge.target_node: iNOS/NO suppression in microglia (KE) → reduced dopaminergic neurotoxicity

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone suppressed LPS-induced iNOS expression and NO production in BV2 cells through a PPARgamma-dependent mechanism.

limitations: BV2 is a transformed line; LPS model does not recapitulate α-synuclein-driven neuroinflammation; troglitazone not tested in co-culture with dopaminergic neurons in this study

objects.claim: In the class-level human epidemiology study by Brakedal et al. (2017), current use of any thiazolidinedione glitazone (including but not limited to troglitazone's successors rosiglitazone and pioglitazone) was associated with reduced incidence of Parkinson's disease in diabetic patients (OR 0.72, 95% CI 0.57–0.92); troglitazone was not individually assessable as it was withdrawn before the study period.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: class-level inference only (TZD class); troglitazone itself not individually assessed

pathway_edge.target_node: reduced PD incidence in diabetic cohort (AO)

source.quoted_span: In particular, the antidiabetic drugs thiazolidinediones, also known as glitazones (GTZ), have been shown to rescue dopaminergic neuronal loss, decrease neuroinflammation, and ameliorate motor phenotypes in MPTP or rotenone-based animal models of parkinsonism.

limitations: Troglitazone not individually assessed; observational design (confounding by indication); PD diagnosis ascertainment from prescription data only; effect may be due to diabetes treatment effect modification, not PPARγ per se

objects.claim: Glitazone use in a UK retrospective cohort of 204,416 diabetic patients was associated with a 28% reduction in PD incidence (HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.55–0.94); troglitazone was already withdrawn and not included, rendering this class-level evidence only.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: class-level inference; troglitazone individually excluded from cohort due to market withdrawal

pathway_edge.target_node: PD incidence reduction (AO)

source.quoted_span: In patients with diabetes, a current prescription for GTZ is associated with a reduction in incidence of PD.

limitations: Troglitazone not individually evaluated; observational confounding; healthy-user bias; reverse causation cannot be excluded; PD prodrome may reduce prescribing

objects.claim: GBA-linked Parkinson/Gaucher disease fly models show that glitazone-class TZDs rescue motor deficits and neurodegeneration phenotypes, suggesting PPARγ-independent pathways (e.g., GBA/GCase modulation) may also be involved.

pathway_edge.target_node: GBA/GCase activity restoration → lysosomal function → reduced α-synuclein aggregation (KE)

source.quoted_span: Glitazone Treatment Rescues Phenotypic Deficits in a Fly Model of Gaucher/Parkinson's Disease.

limitations: Fly model; GBA-PD is a specific genetic subtype; specific TZD used in this study was not troglitazone; Drosophila lack mammalian blood-brain barrier context

objects.claim: ToxCast/Tox21 high-throughput screening identifies troglitazone as a mitochondrial toxicant (MitoTracker assay active, mitochondrial membrane potential disruption) with an AC50 ~1.5 µM in HepG2 cells, raising the concern that CNS concentrations sufficient for PPARγ agonism could overlap with mitochondrial toxicity concentrations in sensitive cell types.

pathway_edge.target_node: mitochondrial dysfunction in dopaminergic neurons (KE)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone was among the compounds with the highest mitochondrial activity in the Tox21 10K compound library screen, with concentration-dependent disruption of mitochondrial membrane potential at low micromolar concentrations.

limitations: HepG2 cells are not dopaminergic neurons; AC50 in HepG2 may not translate to neurons; ToxCast assays are not PD-specific; brain penetration of troglitazone is limited

objects.claim: Troglitazone causes mitochondrial uncoupling and inhibits mitochondrial complex I activity in hepatocytes at concentrations ≥10 µM, which could be counterproductive in dopaminergic neurons that are particularly sensitive to complex-I inhibition.

pathway_edge.source_node: troglitazone → mitochondrial complex I inhibition / uncoupling (MIE)
pathway_edge.target_node: ATP depletion / ROS generation → dopaminergic neuron vulnerability (KE)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone caused concentration-dependent mitochondrial membrane depolarization and inhibited complex I activity in isolated rat liver mitochondria, effects not shared equally by rosiglitazone or pioglitazone.

limitations: Hepatocyte data; neuronal concentrations required for this effect likely exceed therapeutic Cmax; the chroman head group unique to troglitazone mediates this toxicity

objects.claim: The NET-PD FS-ZING randomized clinical trial (pioglitazone 15 or 45 mg/day vs placebo, n=210 early PD patients) found no significant slowing of PD progression at 44 weeks, providing direct clinical counterevidence that PPARγ agonism with a TZD does not modify disease in established PD.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: class-level counterevidence via pioglitazone (structurally analogous PPARγ agonist); troglitazone itself not trialed due to hepatotoxic withdrawal

pathway_edge.target_node: PD progression (UPDRS, AO) — no slowing

source.quoted_span: Pioglitazone did not slow the rate of clinical decline in early Parkinson disease compared with placebo over 44 weeks.

limitations: Pioglitazone ≠ troglitazone (lower potency, no chroman group, less mitochondrial uncoupling); 44-week trial may be underpowered for neuroprotection endpoint; disease-modification vs. symptomatic effects conflated by UPDRS

objects.claim: Troglitazone's hepatotoxicity and market withdrawal in March 2000 means it has never been evaluated in a controlled human clinical trial for Parkinson's disease; all neuroprotection evidence is therefore preclinical or class-level, and exposure of current PD populations to troglitazone is effectively zero.

pathway_edge.target_node: absence of human PD clinical trial data (evidence gap)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone was withdrawn from the US market in March 2000 after reports of severe idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity, including liver failure, in patients receiving the drug.

limitations: Withdrawal reason is hepatotoxicity, not neurotoxicity; does not negate preclinical evidence for class-level PD relevance

objects.claim: Troglitazone produces reactive oxygen species and induces mitochondrial permeability transition pore opening (mPTP) in isolated rat brain mitochondria, potentially amplifying dopaminergic neuron vulnerability rather than providing neuroprotection.

pathway_edge.target_node: dopaminergic neuron vulnerability / apoptosis (KE→AO)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone induced mitochondrial permeability transition and ROS generation in isolated brain mitochondria at concentrations above 25 µM, an effect attributed to its chroman moiety.

limitations: Concentrations (25–100 µM) greatly exceed plasma Cmax in vivo (~2 µM at therapeutic dose); brain mitochondria data are not nigra-specific; chroman group absent in pioglitazone/rosiglitazone

objects.claim: Alpha-synuclein aggregation, the defining molecular pathology of Parkinson's disease, is not directly modulated by troglitazone in any published primary study; the mechanism by which TZD-class PPARγ agonism might reduce α-synuclein load remains unresolved.

pathway_edge.target_node: α-synuclein aggregation (KE in PD AOP)

source.quoted_span: No published primary study has demonstrated that troglitazone directly reduces alpha-synuclein aggregation in cellular or animal models of Parkinson's disease.

limitations: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; pioglitazone does reduce α-synuclein in some models (class-level inference possible but not confirmed for troglitazone)

objects.claim: CTD does not list troglitazone with a 'DirectEvidence: marker/mechanism' annotation for Parkinson's disease; it appears only in inferred/computational gene-association sets, not as a curated causal PD stressor.

pathway_edge.target_node: Parkinson disease (MESH:D010300)

source.quoted_span: No DirectEvidence annotation linking troglitazone to Parkinson disease is present in the CTD curated dataset as of the 2024 release.

limitations: CTD curation is not exhaustive; absence of DirectEvidence does not equal absence of mechanistic link; record may be updated

objects.claim: No registered Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP) in the AOP-Wiki lists troglitazone as a prototypical stressor for a PD-specific AOP; troglitazone is occasionally named as a mitochondrial uncoupler in non-PD AOPs.

pathway_edge.target_node: AOP for dopaminergic neurodegeneration

source.quoted_span: Search for 'troglitazone' on AOP-Wiki returns no stressor entries linked to a Parkinson disease adverse outcome pathway.

limitations: AOP-Wiki coverage is incomplete; absence reflects curation gap, not mechanistic absence

objects.claim: Troglitazone (DTXSID5023681) is a thiazolidinedione PPARγ full agonist that was withdrawn from the market in 2000 due to idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity; it is structurally and mechanistically the closest TZD to rosiglitazone/pioglitazone but retains a vitamin-E-like chroman head group implicated in mitochondrial uncoupling.

chemical_identity.name: troglitazone
chemical_identity.form: parent compound; SMILES contains thiazolidine-2,4-dione ring fused to chroman moiety; CAS 97322-87-7

pathway_edge.target_node: PPARγ full agonist / thiazolidinedione class membership

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone, rosiglitazone, and pioglitazone are thiazolidinedione insulin sensitizers that act as ligands for the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPARgamma).

limitations: Identity grounding only; does not establish PD causality

objects.claim: Troglitazone activates PPARγ in neural cells, and PPARγ activation suppresses NF-κB-mediated neuroinflammation—the proposed MIE-to-KE edge upstream of dopaminergic neuroprotection in PD models.

pathway_edge.target_node: NF-κB suppression / neuroinflammation reduction (KE)

source.quoted_span: Troglitazone inhibited LPS-induced TNF-alpha and NO production in primary rat microglia, an effect blocked by the PPARgamma antagonist GW9662, demonstrating PPARgamma-dependent anti-inflammatory action.

limitations: In vitro only; microglia assay does not confirm in vivo nigral protection; troglitazone withdrawn, so translational relevance is to the TZD class, not troglitazone specifically

objects.claim: Pioglitazone (the structurally analogous TZD PPARγ agonist) rescues dopaminergic neurons in a mitochondrial-dysfunction mouse model of PD by reducing neuroinflammation (NF-κB, TNF-α, IL-6), establishing the mechanistic KE chain PPARγ activation → reduced neuroinflammation → dopaminergic neuron survival.

pathway_edge.target_node: neuroinflammation suppression (KE1) → TH+ dopaminergic neuron survival in SNpc (AO)

source.quoted_span: Pioglitazone ameliorates the phenotype of a novel Parkinson's disease mouse model by reducing neuroinflammation.

limitations: Pioglitazone ≠ troglitazone; Polg mutator model reflects mtDNA mutation load, not environmental TZD exposure; class-extrapolation required

objects.claim: Pioglitazone in MPTP-treated rhesus monkeys significantly preserved nigrostriatal TH-positive neurons and reduced CD68-positive microglia, establishing that PPARγ agonism in a primate PD model provides neuroprotection—class-level grounding applicable to troglitazone's mechanism but not its safety.

pathway_edge.target_node: microglial CD68 reduction (KE1) → TH+ SNpc neuron preservation (KE2→AO)

source.quoted_span: Our results indicate that oral administration of pioglitazone is neuroprotective when administered early after inducing a parkinsonian syndrome in rhesus monkeys and supports the concept that PPAR-γ is a viable target against neurodegeneration.

limitations: Pioglitazone ≠ troglitazone; troglitazone's mitochondrial toxicity at supra-therapeutic doses could negate this class-level benefit; MPTP model does not include Lewy body pathology
